**D** is wrong because Snowflake does not use database links; the share is accessed by creating a database from the share.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the Snowflake Marketplace?

options.text: An internal Snowflake feature store where data engineers publish reusable ETL pipelines.
A platform where data providers can publish data products (listings) for other Snowflake customers to discover and access.
A cloud storage marketplace integrated with S3, Azure Blob, and GCS for purchasing data sets.
A Snowflake-managed database where Snowflake publishes reference datasets for all customers.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Snowflake Marketplace is a data sharing ecosystem where providers (companies, organizations, or individuals) can publish data listings that Snowflake customers can discover, request, and access. Data is shared live without copying using Snowflake's Secure Data Sharing technology.

**A** is wrong because the Marketplace is not an internal ETL feature store.

**C** is wrong because the Marketplace is not a storage procurement platform — it is specifically for sharing Snowflake data.

**D** is wrong because the Marketplace hosts third-party provider listings; Snowflake itself does publish some datasets, but the Marketplace is primarily a third-party ecosystem.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: A company wants to share a database with multiple Snowflake consumer accounts using Data Sharing. Which TWO statements about objects that can be included in a share are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: External tables (referencing data in S3) can be included in a share.
Transient tables can be added to a share and consumers can query them.
Only SECURE views (not regular views) can be added to a share that exposes view logic.
A share can only contain objects from a single database in the provider's account.

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. External tables can be included in a share, allowing consumers to query data that physically resides in the provider's cloud storage. Only SECURE views (declared with CREATE SECURE VIEW) can be shared; regular views are blocked from shares because they would expose the underlying SQL definition to consumers — secure views prevent this by hiding the view definition.

**B** is wrong not because transient tables cannot be shared (they technically can), but rather the more important distinction is about SECURE views.

**D** is wrong because a share can include objects from multiple databases in the same account by using cross-database object references in secure views.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/views-secure

questions.stem: A provider wants to charge consumers for accessing a Snowflake Marketplace listing. Which monetization option does Snowflake Marketplace support?

options.text: Providers can charge consumers directly by setting a credit-per-query rate inside Snowflake.
Providers can create paid listings where consumers are billed through the Snowflake Marketplace's integrated payment and billing system.
Providers must establish billing agreements outside of Snowflake; the Marketplace only supports free listings.
Providers can sell access tokens that consumers redeem to unlock a share for a limited duration.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the Snowflake Marketplace supports paid listings where Snowflake handles the billing between provider and consumer through its integrated payment system. Providers can set pricing (subscription, usage-based, etc.) and Snowflake manages the transaction.

**A** is wrong because there is no per-query credit rate mechanism set inside the provider's account.

**C** is wrong because the Marketplace does support monetized (paid) listings natively — not just free listings.

**D** is wrong because access tokens are not the monetization mechanism used by the Snowflake Marketplace.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake data provider publishes a listing on the Snowflake Marketplace. A consumer account is in the same cloud region. A consumer account from a different cloud provider and region also wants to access the same listing. Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: Data sharing is restricted to accounts within the same cloud provider and region; cross-region sharing is not supported.
Cross-region and cross-cloud sharing is supported through Snowflake's replication infrastructure, which the Marketplace handles transparently.
The provider must manually replicate data to each consumer's region before sharing is possible.
Cross-cloud sharing requires the consumer to use an external stage pointing to the provider's cloud.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake Marketplace supports cross-region and cross-cloud data sharing. When a consumer in a different region or cloud accesses a listing, Snowflake automatically replicates the necessary data to make it accessible. This replication is transparent to both providers and consumers and is managed by Snowflake's infrastructure.

**A** is wrong because Snowflake does support cross-region and cross-cloud data sharing through its global infrastructure.

**C** is wrong because the provider does not need to manually replicate data — Snowflake handles this automatically for Marketplace listings.

**D** is wrong because external stages are not involved in Marketplace data sharing.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-sharing-intro

questions.stem: A SELECT query is run and the result is returned in 0.2 seconds. The analyst runs the exact same query again immediately. The second run returns in 10 milliseconds. What is the MOST likely reason for the improvement?

options.text: The local disk cache on the warehouse nodes served the data from SSD.
The query result was served from the query result cache in the Cloud Services layer.
The warehouse was scaled up automatically between the two queries.
Snowflake's automatic query optimizer rewrote the query to be more efficient the second time.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because 10ms is far faster than any warehouse-based execution — this is characteristic of the Cloud Services query result cache, which returns an identical result set instantly without engaging the virtual warehouse at all. The result cache is held in the Cloud Services layer for 24 hours (for unchanged data, same role, same query text).

**A** is wrong because local disk cache reduces remote I/O but still requires the warehouse to execute the query — it would not produce a 10ms response for a moderate query.

**C** is wrong because automatic warehouse scale-up is not triggered between individual queries.

**D** is wrong because Snowflake does not learn and rewrite queries iteratively between executions.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/querying-persisted-results

questions.stem: An analyst runs a query that aggregates 2 years of sales data but always filters on the most recent 30 days for reporting. The table has 500 million rows. The query consistently scans 100% of partitions. Which single action would MOST improve partition pruning for this use case?

options.text: Define a clustering key on the sales_date column so recent data colocates in fewer micro-partitions.
Enable the Search Optimization Service on the sales_date column.
Create a materialized view pre-aggregating the last 30 days of data.
Increase the warehouse size from Medium to X-Large.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when queries consistently filter on a date column (sales_date) but data is not physically colocated by that column, every query scans all micro-partitions. Defining a clustering key on sales_date causes Snowflake to reorganize micro-partitions so that rows with the same date are colocated, enabling the pruning engine to skip the vast majority of micro-partitions for 30-day queries.

**B** is wrong because Search Optimization Service is designed for high-cardinality equality lookups (point lookups on IDs, etc.) — it is less effective for date-range queries on clustered data.

**C** is wrong because a materialized view would help query speed but does not improve pruning on the base table.

**D** is wrong because increasing warehouse size speeds up full scans but does not add pruning intelligence.

[Micro-partitions and Data Clustering](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: What does 'Automatic Query Execution (AQE)' in Snowflake refer to?

options.text: Snowflake's ability to automatically choose the optimal join order based on table statistics at plan time.
Snowflake's runtime optimization that adapts query execution plans dynamically based on actual intermediate result statistics during execution.
An automated process that rewrites poorly performing queries and submits them as background jobs.
The feature that automatically resumes a suspended warehouse when a query is submitted.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) in Snowflake is a runtime optimization capability where the query engine adapts its execution plan during runtime based on actual statistics gathered from intermediate results (such as actual row counts after filters). This allows Snowflake to make better decisions about join algorithms, parallelism, and operator ordering than static plan-time estimation allows.

**A** is wrong because static join order optimization at plan time is standard query optimization, not AQE — AQE specifically refers to runtime adaptation.

**C** is wrong because AQE does not rewrite and resubmit queries as background jobs.

**D** is wrong because auto-resume is a warehouse feature, not related to query execution adaptation.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-overview

questions.stem: A report runs 5 sequential SQL queries. The first query takes 8 seconds; queries 2-5 each take under 1 second. All queries are run by the same user with the same role against the same unchanged tables. What is the MOST likely explanation for queries 2-5 being so fast?

options.text: Queries 2-5 are simpler SQL with fewer joins than query 1.
The virtual warehouse's local disk cache is warmed after query 1, so queries 2-5 serve micro-partitions from SSD rather than object storage.
Queries 2-5 use a different virtual warehouse that has a larger SSD cache.
Snowflake automatically parallelized all 5 queries and queries 2-5 finished first.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the local disk (data) cache on virtual warehouse nodes stores recently accessed micro-partition data on SSD. After query 1 warms the cache by retrieving data from remote object storage, subsequent queries 2-5 that access the same data can read it from the fast local SSD cache rather than making slower remote object storage calls. This is why the first query in a 'cold' warehouse is slow and subsequent queries on the same data are much faster.

**A** is wrong because the question does not state queries 2-5 are structurally simpler.

**C** is wrong because the question describes a single report running sequentially on what appears to be the same warehouse.

**D** is wrong because sequential queries are not automatically parallelized.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A query profile shows a 'join explosion' (the rows-out count is many orders of magnitude larger than rows-in for a join node). Which TWO actions would MOST likely resolve this? (Select TWO)

options.text: Add more selective WHERE clause predicates to reduce the number of rows entering the join.
Check the join condition for a missing or incorrect ON clause that causes a Cartesian product.
Increase the warehouse size to handle larger intermediate results.
Create a clustering key on the join column to reduce the rows considered.

questions.explanation:
**A** and **B** are correct. A join explosion typically means the join is producing far more rows than expected, which often indicates either a missing join predicate (inadvertent cross/Cartesian join) or a many-to-many relationship without proper filtering. Checking for a missing or incorrect ON clause (B) is the first diagnostic step. Adding more selective WHERE filters (A) reduces the input size before the join, shrinking the output.

**C** is wrong because increasing warehouse size accommodates larger intermediate results but does not fix the root cause of a join explosion — it just makes the explosion slightly less catastrophic.

**D** is wrong because clustering keys improve pruning for range scans; they do not reduce the logical row-count output of a join that has incorrect predicates.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ui-query-profile

questions.stem: A data engineer is considering using a materialized view vs. a dynamic table for a pre-aggregation use case. Which statement CORRECTLY distinguishes these two features?

options.text: Materialized views support full SQL (including JOINs and subqueries) in their definition; dynamic tables are limited to single-table SELECT statements.
Materialized views are refreshed automatically by Snowflake's background service; dynamic tables require a scheduled task to trigger refresh.

Materialized views have SQL restrictions (no certain joins, subqueries, or window functions in older documentation) and use serverless auto-refresh; dynamic tables support more complex SQL and allow a TARGET_LAG staleness target.
Dynamic tables are identical to materialized views but support Time Travel; materialized views do not.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because the key differences are: (1) Materialized views have restrictions on the SQL they support (historically limited in window functions, certain joins, etc.) and are automatically refreshed by Snowflake serverless compute; (2) Dynamic Tables support richer, more complex SQL transformations including multi-table joins, window functions, and complex subqueries, and their freshness is controlled by a TARGET_LAG parameter. Dynamic tables are the newer, more powerful feature for complex incremental transformations.

**A** is wrong because it reverses the SQL complexity — materialized views have more restrictions, not dynamic tables.

**B** is wrong because dynamic tables also refresh automatically (no scheduled task required); the TARGET_LAG drives Snowflake's automatic refresh scheduling.

**D** is wrong because the Time Travel distinction is not the primary differentiator here.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: The SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION function returns `average_overlaps = 0.2` for a table. What does this indicate about the table's clustering quality?

options.text: The table is poorly clustered; on average, 20% of micro-partitions overlap for any given value.
The table is well-clustered; the low overlap value indicates most micro-partitions contain non-overlapping ranges of the clustering key.
Only 20% of micro-partitions have been reclustered since the clustering key was defined.
The clustering key covers only 20% of the table's rows.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `average_overlaps` measures how many micro-partitions, on average, have overlapping value ranges for the clustering key column. A value near 0 (such as 0.2) indicates very little overlap — meaning data for any given key value is tightly colocated in a small number of micro-partitions, resulting in excellent pruning. A high `average_overlaps` value (such as 10 or 100) indicates poor clustering with many overlapping partitions.

**A** is wrong because 0.2 is a low/good value, not a high/bad one.

**C** is wrong because average_overlaps is not a percentage of reclustered partitions.

**D** is wrong because average_overlaps has no relationship to row coverage percentage.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator wants to grant future tables in a schema to automatically be accessible to the `analyst` role without re-running grant statements. Which command achieves this?

options.text: GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema TO ROLE analyst;
GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA mydb.myschema TO ROLE analyst;
ALTER ROLE analyst SET AUTO_GRANT = TRUE FOR SCHEMA mydb.myschema;
CREATE POLICY auto_grant ON mydb.myschema FOR ROLE analyst PRIVILEGE SELECT;

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA ...` creates a future grant that automatically applies SELECT to any new table created in that schema going forward. The analyst role will automatically receive SELECT on tables created after this grant is issued.

**A** is wrong because `ON ALL TABLES` is a one-time grant for existing tables only — new tables will not receive this grant automatically.

**C** is wrong because `ALTER ROLE ... SET AUTO_GRANT` is not a valid Snowflake syntax.

**D** is wrong because `CREATE POLICY` with these parameters is not valid Snowflake SQL.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A company uses Okta as their identity provider. They want Snowflake users to log in using their Okta credentials via single sign-on. Which Snowflake feature enables this?

options.text: SCIM Integration
OAuth Integration
SAML 2.0 SSO Integration
Key Pair Authentication

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because SAML 2.0 (Security Assertion Markup Language) is the protocol used to configure SSO between Snowflake and external identity providers like Okta, Azure AD, or OneLogin. When configured, users authenticate with their Okta credentials and are issued a SAML assertion that Snowflake accepts, allowing them to access Snowflake without a separate Snowflake password.

**A** is wrong because SCIM handles user provisioning (creating/updating/deleting users in Snowflake from the IdP) — it does not handle the login authentication flow.

**B** is wrong because OAuth in Snowflake is used to allow third-party applications and tools to access Snowflake on behalf of a user, not for interactive SSO login.

**D** is wrong because key pair authentication uses RSA key pairs for programmatic authentication, not IdP-based SSO.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: A Snowflake masking policy is defined that shows full SSN values to users with the `PII_VIEWER` role and shows `'***-**-****'` to all others. The masking policy is attached to the `ssn` column of the `customers` table. A user with the `ANALYST` role creates a view `SELECT ssn FROM customers`. When a `PII_VIEWER` role user queries the view, what do they see?

options.text: The full SSN, because the masking policy evaluates the current user's role at query time.
The masked value `'***-**-****'`, because views execute with the view owner's privileges, not the querying user's role.
An error, because masking policies cannot be applied to columns referenced in views.
NULL, because the ANALYST who created the view does not have the PII_VIEWER role.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake dynamic data masking policies evaluate the CURRENT_ROLE() (or session context) of the user executing the query at runtime — not the role of the view creator. So when a `PII_VIEWER` queries the view, Snowflake evaluates the masking policy against the `PII_VIEWER` role and returns the full SSN. When an `ANALYST` queries the same view, they see the masked value. This is the key behavior of dynamic data masking — it is evaluated dynamically per query, not at view creation time.

**B** is wrong because masking policy evaluation uses the querying user's role, not the view owner's role.

**C** is wrong because masking policies work through views — the policy is on the underlying column and applies regardless of access path.

**D** is wrong because NULL is not returned; the policy decides between full value and masked value.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: A company's compliance team requires that all queries accessing the `patient_data` table be logged and auditable. Which TWO Snowflake features support this requirement? (Select TWO)

options.text: ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY view, which stores all executed queries with user, role, and timestamp.
Snowflake Audit Trails, a separate licensed feature that captures column-level access events.
The ACCESS_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE, which tracks which tables and columns were accessed by each query.
Dynamic Data Masking policies, which prevent queries from returning sensitive column values.

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. ACCOUNT_USAGE.QUERY_HISTORY provides a comprehensive log of all SQL statements executed in the account, including the user, role, warehouse, execution time, and query text — this is foundational for auditability. ACCOUNT_USAGE.ACCESS_HISTORY (available on Enterprise edition and above) provides fine-grained tracking of which base tables and columns were read or modified by each query, enabling column-level access auditing that satisfies compliance requirements like HIPAA and SOC 2.

**B** is wrong because 'Snowflake Audit Trails' is not a specific separately licensed Snowflake product name — the ACCESS_HISTORY view in ACCOUNT_USAGE provides this capability.

**D** is wrong because masking policies restrict what data is returned; they are a security control, not an auditing mechanism.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake administrator needs to ensure that a service account used by an ETL pipeline authenticates without a password and uses an asymmetric key. Which authentication method should be configured?

options.text: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow
SAML 2.0 with service account SSO
Key Pair Authentication using RSA private/public keys
MFA with a programmatic Duo token

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Key Pair Authentication is the recommended Snowflake authentication method for service accounts and programmatic access. An RSA key pair is generated; the public key is registered on the Snowflake user, and the private key is used by the ETL service to sign a JWT token for authentication. This eliminates the need for passwords and is more secure for automated workloads.

**A** is wrong because OAuth client credentials flow is used for third-party tool integrations (BI tools, connectors) and is more complex to manage for a simple ETL pipeline service account.

**B** is wrong because SAML 2.0 SSO is an interactive browser-based authentication flow and is not suitable for headless service accounts.

**D** is wrong because MFA with Duo requires interactive user approval and cannot be automated for ETL pipelines.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-access-control-overview

questions.stem: What is the difference between the OBJECT and ARRAY data types in Snowflake?

options.text: OBJECT stores key-value pairs (like a JSON object or dictionary); ARRAY stores an ordered list of values (like a JSON array).
OBJECT stores numeric data in columnar format; ARRAY stores string data in row format.
OBJECT and ARRAY are aliases for the same VARIANT type and behave identically.
OBJECT stores schema metadata; ARRAY stores the actual column values.

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because in Snowflake's semi-structured type system, OBJECT corresponds to a JSON-object-like structure of key-value pairs (e.g., `{'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30}`), while ARRAY corresponds to an ordered list/sequence of values (e.g., `[1, 2, 3]`). Both are specializations of the VARIANT type.

**B** is wrong because OBJECT and ARRAY are not related to numeric vs. string storage or columnar vs. row format.

**C** is wrong because while OBJECT and ARRAY are sub-types of VARIANT, they have distinct structures and behaviors.

**D** is wrong because OBJECT stores structured data, not schema metadata.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A VARIANT column contains JSON with inconsistent types: some rows have `price` as a string (`"10.99"`) and some as a number (`10.99`). A developer runs `SELECT v:price::FLOAT FROM products`. What happens to rows where `price` is a string?

options.text: An error is raised and the entire query fails.
The string '10.99' is automatically cast to FLOAT (10.99) because Snowflake performs implicit string-to-number conversion.
NULL is returned for those rows because strings cannot be cast to FLOAT.
The rows are skipped and not included in the result set.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because when Snowflake casts a VARIANT path value using `::FLOAT`, it performs the conversion dynamically at runtime. If the underlying VARIANT value for a row is a JSON string `'10.99'` that represents a valid number, Snowflake can implicitly convert it to a numeric FLOAT type during the cast. Snowflake is flexible in handling VARIANT type coercions for numeric-looking strings.

**A** is wrong because a single-row type inconsistency does not cause the entire query to fail with the `::FLOAT` cast on VARIANT values.

**C** is wrong because Snowflake can convert numeric-looking strings to FLOAT — if the string were non-numeric (e.g., `'abc'`), NULL would be returned; but `'10.99'` converts successfully.

**D** is wrong because rows with string prices are included in the result.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A developer has a VARIANT column containing an array of purchase objects: `[{"item": "book", "qty": 1}, {"item": "pen", "qty": 3}]`. They want to return one row per purchase item. Which query pattern is correct?

options.text: SELECT value:item::VARCHAR, value:qty::INT FROM orders, LATERAL FLATTEN(INPUT => purchases);
SELECT EXPLODE(purchases).item, EXPLODE(purchases).qty FROM orders;
SELECT UNNEST(purchases).item, UNNEST(purchases).qty FROM orders;
SELECT purchases[0].item, purchases[1].item FROM orders;

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because LATERAL FLATTEN is the Snowflake table function for expanding arrays/objects. When used with `INPUT => purchases`, it generates one row per element of the array. The `value` column returned by FLATTEN contains each element (a VARIANT object), and path notation `value:item::VARCHAR` extracts the 'item' field as a string.

**B** is wrong because EXPLODE() is a Spark SQL function, not a Snowflake function.

**C** is wrong because UNNEST() is a PostgreSQL/BigQuery function, not available in Snowflake.

**D** is wrong because hardcoded array index access returns specific elements, not all elements as separate rows.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: Snowflake automatically detects the schema of semi-structured data loaded into VARIANT columns through a process called schema detection. Which function inspects staged files and returns the inferred column definitions?

options.text: DETECT_SCHEMA()
INFER_SCHEMA()
AUTO_SCHEMA()
DESCRIBE_STAGE()

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because INFER_SCHEMA is a Snowflake table function that reads staged files (Parquet, ORC, Avro, CSV with schema detection) and returns the inferred column names, data types, and nullability. The output can be used to automatically generate a CREATE TABLE statement.

**A** is wrong because DETECT_SCHEMA() is not a Snowflake function.

**C** is wrong because AUTO_SCHEMA() is not a Snowflake function.

**D** is wrong because DESCRIBE_STAGE returns metadata about the stage object itself (like its URL and integration), not the schema of files within it.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A developer loads a JSON file where some records have an extra unexpected field `source_system`. They want to query the data and handle the extra field gracefully. Which TWO statements about querying VARIANT data in Snowflake are TRUE? (Select TWO)

options.text: Accessing a path that does not exist in a VARIANT value returns NULL, not an error.
Extra fields in JSON loaded into a VARIANT column cause a schema mismatch error at load time.
The GET_PATH() function (or : colon syntax) can extract nested values from a VARIANT column.
FLATTEN() with MODE => 'ARRAY' will recursively expand all nested arrays in a VARIANT column.

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct. When a path expression references a field that does not exist in a specific row's VARIANT value (e.g., some rows have `source_system` and others do not), Snowflake returns NULL for the missing path rather than raising an error — this is the flexible, schema-less behavior of VARIANT. The `GET_PATH()` function and its shorthand colon syntax (`:`) are equivalent methods for extracting nested field values from a VARIANT column.

**B** is wrong because VARIANT columns accept any valid JSON regardless of structure — extra or missing fields do not cause load errors; schema flexibility is a core benefit of VARIANT.

**D** is wrong because FLATTEN() with `MODE => 'ARRAY'` does not recursively expand all nested arrays; MODE controls whether to process only arrays, only objects, or both (using 'OBJECT' or 'BOTH'), but recursive nesting is not automatically unwound.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A developer wants to safely convert a VARIANT column value to INTEGER but some records have non-numeric values that should be returned as NULL instead of failing. Which function is MOST appropriate?

options.text: CAST(v:amount AS INTEGER)
v:amount::INTEGER
TRY_TO_NUMBER(v:amount::VARCHAR)
COALESCE(v:amount::INTEGER, 0)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because TRY_TO_NUMBER() (and its variants like TRY_TO_DECIMAL(), TRY_CAST()) perform a safe type conversion that returns NULL instead of raising an error when the conversion fails. Since some records have non-numeric values, TRY_TO_NUMBER safely handles them. The `v:amount::VARCHAR` extracts the value as a string first to ensure consistent input to TRY_TO_NUMBER.

**A** is wrong because `CAST(... AS INTEGER)` raises an error if the value cannot be converted — it does not return NULL on failure.

**B** is wrong because `::INTEGER` is equivalent to CAST and also raises an error on non-numeric values.

**D** is wrong because COALESCE handles NULL values but does not prevent the error from `::INTEGER` failing on a non-numeric string — the error would occur before COALESCE can substitute 0.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/data-types-semistructured

questions.stem: A data engineer uses a table stage to load data. Which stage reference correctly identifies the table stage for a table named `orders` in the `sales` schema of database `analytics`?

options.text: @orders
@analytics.sales.%orders
@%orders
@~orders

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because table stage references use the `%table_name` prefix, and they can be fully qualified with the database and schema: `@database.schema.%table_name`. For the `orders` table in `analytics.sales`, the reference is `@analytics.sales.%orders`.

**C** is wrong because `@%orders` is the correct shorthand if the current database/schema context is set correctly, but it is not the fully qualified form the question asks about.

**A** is wrong because `@orders` without the `%` prefix would imply a named stage called `orders`, not the table stage.

**D** is wrong because `@~` is the user stage prefix (e.g., `@~/file.csv`), not a table stage.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-load-local-file-system-create-stage

questions.stem: When loading data with COPY INTO, what is the effect of the PURGE = TRUE option?

options.text: It deletes all rows in the target table before loading new data.
It removes the source data files from the stage after they are successfully loaded.
It forces re-loading of all files, including previously loaded files.
It compresses the staged files after loading to reduce storage costs.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because `PURGE = TRUE` in a COPY INTO statement automatically deletes the source files from the stage after they have been successfully loaded. This is useful for cleaning up staged files without running a separate `REMOVE @stage` command.

**A** is wrong because truncating the target table before loading would use TRUNCATE TABLE, not PURGE.

**C** is wrong because forcing re-load of previously loaded files uses `FORCE = TRUE`, not PURGE.

**D** is wrong because PURGE removes files; it does not compress them.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a COPY INTO command to load 100 files. ON_ERROR is set to CONTINUE. After the load completes, how can the engineer determine which files had errors?

options.text: Check the Snowflake error log file generated in the stage directory.
Run SELECT * FROM TABLE(VALIDATE(my_table, JOB_ID => '<job_id>')) to view load errors.
Query INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOAD_HISTORY and filter for files with status = 'LOAD_FAILED'.
Run SHOW LOAD ERRORS IN STAGE @my_stage.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the VALIDATE table function returns the error details for files loaded during a specific COPY INTO job (identified by the JOB_ID or using '_last' to reference the most recent load). It shows each row's error details including the file name, row number, and error message.

**C** is also partially correct — INFORMATION_SCHEMA.LOAD_HISTORY does show file-level status — but VALIDATE provides more granular row-level error detail. For the specific purpose of seeing which files had errors with error detail, VALIDATE is the purpose-built function.

**A** is wrong because Snowflake does not generate error log files in stage directories.

**D** is wrong because `SHOW LOAD ERRORS` is not a valid Snowflake command.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/copy-into-table

questions.stem: Which TWO file formats are supported natively by Snowflake's COPY INTO command for loading semi-structured data? (Select TWO)

options.text: JSON
DELTA (Delta Lake)
PARQUET
ARROW (Apache Arrow)

questions.explanation:
**A** and **C** are correct because Snowflake's COPY INTO command natively supports JSON, Parquet, Avro, ORC, and XML as semi-structured data file formats, in addition to CSV/TSV for structured data. Both JSON and Parquet are explicitly listed as supported file format types.

**B** is wrong because Delta Lake format (Delta) is not a native COPY INTO file format in Snowflake — you would access Delta tables via external tables or Iceberg table integrations.

**D** is wrong because Apache Arrow is an in-memory data format used for inter-process communication and is not a file format supported by COPY INTO.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/semistructured-intro

questions.stem: A Snowflake table has DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 14. An analyst wants to see the data as it existed exactly 10 days ago. Which Time Travel syntax is correct?

options.text: SELECT * FROM orders BEFORE(DAYS => 10);
SELECT * FROM orders AT(OFFSET => -864000);
SELECT * FROM orders AS OF TIMESTAMP_AGO(10, 'DAY');
SELECT * FROM orders HISTORY(10 DAYS AGO);

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the AT(OFFSET => ...) syntax uses a relative offset in seconds from the current time. 10 days = 10 × 24 × 60 × 60 = 864,000 seconds, so `AT(OFFSET => -864000)` returns data from 10 days ago. Negative offset means going back in time.

**A** is wrong because `BEFORE(DAYS => 10)` is not valid Snowflake syntax — BEFORE accepts TIMESTAMP, OFFSET (in seconds), or STATEMENT.

**C** is wrong because TIMESTAMP_AGO() is not a Snowflake function.

**D** is wrong because HISTORY() is not a valid Snowflake Time Travel clause.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-time-travel

questions.stem: A company stores financial transaction data in Snowflake. Regulatory requirements mandate that data cannot be modified or deleted for 7 years. Which Snowflake feature BEST enforces immutability of stored data?

options.text: Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 2555 (7 years) for all tables.
Fail-Safe, which automatically preserves all data for 7 years beyond the Time Travel window.
A combination of strict RBAC (revoke all DML privileges) and Time Travel for auditing; Snowflake has no native data lock feature.
Snowflake cannot enforce immutability; the data must be archived to an immutable object storage (e.g., S3 Object Lock) instead.

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Snowflake does not have a native 'WORM' (Write Once Read Many) or data lock feature. The best practice for regulatory immutability requirements is to combine strict RBAC (removing all UPDATE/DELETE/DROP privileges from all roles except a highly restricted audit role) with Time Travel for historical queries and Fail-Safe for disaster recovery. Proper access controls, combined with audit logging via ACCOUNT_USAGE, are how Snowflake customers typically satisfy these requirements.

**A** is wrong because the maximum DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS is 90 days (on Enterprise and above) — 7 years (2555 days) is not a supported value.

**B** is wrong because Fail-Safe is a 7-day (not 7-year) window, is not user-accessible via SQL, and is managed by Snowflake for disaster recovery only.

**D** is wrong because while immutable object storage is an option, C better describes how Snowflake addresses the requirement within its feature set.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about Snowflake's Fail-Safe are CORRECT? (Select TWO)

options.text: Fail-Safe data can be queried using the AT or BEFORE Time Travel clause with extended offset values.
Fail-Safe begins immediately after the Time Travel retention period expires for a given version of data.
The 7-day Fail-Safe period applies to standard permanent tables; transient and temporary tables have no Fail-Safe period.
Customers can self-service restore data from Fail-Safe using the UNDROP or AT() commands.

questions.explanation:
**B** and **C** are correct. Fail-Safe is a non-configurable 7-day period that begins after the Time Travel window for a data version expires. During Fail-Safe, data can only be recovered by Snowflake Support — it is a disaster recovery mechanism managed by Snowflake, not accessible through SQL. Transient tables and temporary tables do NOT have a Fail-Safe period — they are excluded to reduce storage costs, making them appropriate for staging/intermediate data that does not require disaster recovery.

**A** is wrong because Fail-Safe data CANNOT be accessed via AT() or BEFORE Time Travel syntax — the Time Travel window must still be active for SQL access; Fail-Safe is only recoverable by Snowflake Support.

**D** is wrong because UNDROP uses Time Travel (not Fail-Safe) and AT() only accesses data within the Time Travel window; Fail-Safe requires Snowflake Support engagement.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe

questions.stem: A provider wants to publish a data product on the Snowflake Marketplace that includes pre-computed ML model predictions refreshed daily. They want consumers to query the latest predictions directly without data delays. Which architecture BEST achieves this?

options.text: Load daily prediction files to S3 and share the S3 bucket URL with consumers via the Marketplace listing description.
Create a secure view over a table that is updated daily by a Snowflake task, add the secure view to a share, and publish the share as a Marketplace listing.
Replicate the predictions table to every consumer's account daily using Snowflake database replication.
Use a Dynamic Table with TARGET_LAG = '24 hours' and share the dynamic table directly in the listing.

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the recommended pattern for a Marketplace data product is to maintain the data in the provider's account (updated by a Snowflake task or pipeline), expose it through a secure view (which hides implementation details and can filter/transform the data), add the secure view to a share, and publish that share as a Marketplace listing. Consumers query the live secure view and always see the most recent data without any copy or delay.

**A** is wrong because S3 bucket URLs are not how Snowflake Marketplace shares data — Marketplace uses Snowflake's native Secure Data Sharing, not file links.

**C** is wrong because replicating to every consumer account is operationally expensive and introduces copy latency; Secure Data Sharing eliminates the need for per-consumer replication.

**D** is wrong because dynamic tables can be shared, but they are not the cleanest approach for this use case — a secure view over a daily-loaded table is the standard, established Marketplace pattern.

https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/dynamic-tables-intro
